full_response:
We compute the cross product \(\mathbf{v}(\lambda) \times \mathbf{v}'(\lambda)\) in Cartesian coordinates, where  
\[
\mathbf{v}(\lambda) = \left( \sin(\lambda^2),\, \ln(\lambda+1),\, 2\lambda^2-1 \right).
\]  
First, find the derivative:  
\[
\mathbf{v}'(\lambda) = \left( 2\lambda \cos(\lambda^2),\, \frac{1}{\lambda+1},\, 4\lambda \right).
\]  
The cross product is given by  
\[
\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{v}' = \begin{vmatrix}
\mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\
\sin(\lambda^2) & \ln(\lambda+1) & 2\lambda^2-1 \\
2\lambda \cos(\lambda^2) & \frac{1}{\lambda+1} & 4\lambda
\end{vmatrix}.
\]  
Computing each component:  

- \(\mathbf{i}\)-component:  
\[
\ln(\lambda+1) \cdot 4\lambda - (2\lambda^2-1) \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda+1} = 4\lambda \ln(\lambda+1) - \frac{2\lambda^2-1}{\lambda+1}.
\]  

- \(\mathbf{j}\)-component:  
\[
-\left[ \sin(\lambda^2) \cdot 4\lambda - (2\lambda^2-1) \cdot 2\lambda \cos(\lambda^2) \right] = -4\lambda \sin(\lambda^2) + 2\lambda(2\lambda^2-1) \cos(\lambda^2).
\]  

- \(\mathbf{k}\)-component:  
\[
\sin(\lambda^2) \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda+1} - \ln(\lambda+1) \cdot 2\lambda \cos(\lambda^2) = \frac{\sin(\lambda^2)}{\lambda+1} - 2\lambda \ln(\lambda+1) \cos(\lambda^2).
\]  

Thus,  
\[
\mathbf{v}(\lambda) \times \mathbf{v}'(\lambda) = \left( 4\lambda \ln(\lambda+1) - \frac{2\lambda^2-1}{\lambda+1},\; -4\lambda \sin(\lambda^2) + 2\lambda(2\lambda^2-1) \cos(\lambda^2),\; \frac{\sin(\lambda^2)}{\lambda+1} - 2\lambda \ln(\lambda+1) \cos(\lambda^2) \right).
\]

\boxed{\left( 4\lambda \ln(\lambda+1) - \dfrac{2\lambda^2-1}{\lambda+1},\; -4\lambda \sin(\lambda^2) + 2\lambda(2\lambda^2-1) \cos(\lambda^2),\; \dfrac{\sin(\lambda^2)}{\lambda+1} - 2\lambda \ln(\lambda+1) \cos(\lambda^2) \right)}  

Confidence: 100%
